titleStmt.title: Letter from Patrick Langford Beazley to Piaras Béaslaí , 27 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Patrick Langford Beazley

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter to Piaras Béaslaí (born Percy Frederick Beazley; 1881–1965) from his father, Patrick Langford Beazley. After enquiring after Piaras's sea journey to Dublin, Patrick writes about the increased employment opportunities for women. He claims that they are getting a good wage but not as high as the men they have replaced. Béaslaí was an English-born, influential member of the IRB. In February of 1916 he published the Fáinne, a publication to organise Gaelic speakers. He soon abandoned it and became involved in politics and would later fight in the Easter Rising.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Glengariff, Rudgrave Square, Egremont, Cheshire, 27th Jan., 1916. My dearest Pierce, I hope you got through
your journey to Dublin
allright. We were hoping
to have a line from you;
I have no doubt it will
come in a day or so. The
weather was, I think, pleasâ
ant for travelling, and I
dare say the sea was fairly
calm. If you had gone
the previous day you would
have found it pretty rough, if
one may judge from the conâ
dition of the waves. We are
both well, thank God, but have
no news. There can be no
doubt that the women here
are taking advantage of the
chances offered them. They are
crowding into all the outâ
door employments in which
their services are demanded and
in a great many of the offiâ
ces they are performing the
work of clerks and bookâ
keepers. They are getting very
good wages, but not such high
pay as the men whom they reâ
place got. It is probable there
will be ructions on the subject
after the war. Take good care of your health
and write. With fondest love from mother and myself,
Ever affectionately Father.
